summary: Jeff Atwood responds to the controversy surrounding his earlier 'Please Don't Learn to Code' post by clarifying his actual position: he doesn't oppose learning to code, but opposes treating coding as a universal requirement or silver bullet. He argues that programming should emerge naturally from pursuing interesting problems rather than being learned for its own sake. Using the story of a 45-year-old attorney who discovered joy in building complex database solutions, Atwood illustrates that the path to programming should be through passion and genuine interest. He concludes that the only qualification needed to become a programmer is to 'fall in love with code.'
key_points: Coding skills should emerge from pursuing problems you find interesting, not from treating programming as inherently valuable
The history of computing has been about freeing people from needing to code, so universal coding mandates go backwards
Basic internet literacy doesn't require everyone to understand how computers work, just as driving doesn't require understanding auto mechanics
The specific technologies and languages of today may be unrecognizable in 20-30 years, making particular approaches less important than fundamentals
True programmers recognize fellow believers who are genuinely passionate about code, regardless of formal credentials
money_quotes: I'd argue that the people who need to learn to code will be spurred on most of all by honesty, not religious faith in the truthiness of code as a universal good.

I view the entire arc of software development as a field where we programmers spend our lives writing code so that our fellow human beings no longer need to write code (or even worse, become programmers) to get things done with computers.
I'd prefer that people spend their time discovering what problems they love and find interesting, first, and researching the hell out of those problems.
What I advocate is shamelessly following your joy.
I didn't become a programmer because someone told me learning to code was important, I became a programmer because I wanted to change the rules of the video games I was playing.
If you want to be a programmer, all you need to do is follow your joy and fall in love with code.
Any programmer worth their salt immediately recognizes a fellow true believer, a person as madly in love with code as they are, warts and all.
key_insight: The path to becoming a programmer should be paved by genuine passion for solving interesting problems, not by treating coding as a mandatory skill everyone must acquire.
